text: Studies indicate they should be able to produce electricity in 1000-Mw(e) stations for about 2.6 mills/kwhr in investor-owned utilities, a cost that is as low or lower than projected for advanced converter reactors or fast breeder power stations.
Development, construction, and startup of the breeder reactor is estimated to take about eight years and to cost about $125 million.

The molten-salt breeder, though seeming to be a by-way in reactor development, in fact represents the culmination of more than 17 years of research and development.
In 1958 the most prominent fluid fuel projects were the liquid bismuth reactor, the aqueous homogeneous reactor, and the molten-salt reactor.
In 1959 the AEC assembled a task force to evaluate the three concepts.
The principal conclusion of their report<sup>1</sup> was that the "molten-salt reactor has the highest probability of achieving technical feasibility."
This verdict of the 1959 task force appears to be confirmed by the operation of the Molten-Salt Reactor Experiment.
We estimate that a 1000-Mw(e) molten-salt breeder should cost $115 per kilowatt (electric) and that the fuel cycle cost ought to be in the range of 0.3 to 0.4 mill/kwhr(e).
The overall cost of power from a privately owned, 1000-Mw(e) Molten-Salt Breeder Reactor should come to around 2.6 mills/kwhr(e).

The molten-salt breeder, operating in the thermal Th-233U cycle, is characterized by a low breeding ratio: the maximum breeding ratio consistent with low fuel cycle costs is estimated to be about 1.07.
Whereas the specific inventory of the fast reactor ranges between 2.5 to $5\mathrm{kg/Mw(e)}$ , the specific inventory of the molten-salt breeder ranges between 0.4 to $1.0\mathrm{kg/Mw(e)}$ .
The estimated fuel doubling time for the MSBR therefore falls in the range of 8 to 50 years.
This is comparable to estimates of doubling times of 7 to 30 years given in fast breeder reactor design studies.
A projection of the rate of growth of the electrical generating capacity in the U.S. is presented in Table 1.

Numbers through the year 2000 were based on estimates developed by the Federal Power Commission and the AEC for the Report to the President in 1962 and were the nuclear capacities updated to reflect the present rapid growth of nuclear electric capacity.

The total capacities for the years beyond 2000 were based, in Case A, on continued growth at the exponential rate of about $5\%$ per year and, in Case B, on continued growth at a linear rate of 100,000 Mw/yr-- the rate at year 2000.
In Case B, the rate of expansion of total electrical generating capacity would be down to about $2\%$ per year by the year 2030.
The nuclear capacities for the years beyond 2000 were extrapolated on the basis that all new generating capacity after about 2020 would be nuclear.
Case A - exponential growth continued at rate of about $5\%$ per year beyond 2000.
Case B - growth linear after 2000 at a rate of 100,000 Mw per year.

The efficiency of fuel utilization is determined by the quantity of $\mathrm{U}_{3}\mathrm{O}_{8}$ required to provide the total inventory of fissionable material associated with the reactor per megawatt of electrical generating capacity and the quantity of $\mathrm{U}_{3}\mathrm{O}_{8}$ required per year per megawatt of electrical generating capacity to provide for burnup of fissionable material.
These requirements are listed in Table 3 for several types of reactors.

section: Fuel Cycle and Total Power Costs
text: In view of the many uncertainties, we interpret the data in Table 5 to indicate primarily that a molten-salt thermal breeder plant could produce power at a cost competitive with the cost of power from a fast breeder plant and with far less dependence on the sale of fissionable material.
1000-Mw(e) MOLTEN-SALT THERMAL BREEDER POWER PLANT
Studies of the conceptual design of a 1000-Mw(e) molten-salt thermal breeder power plant (MSBR) and of some alternatives or improvements are reported in ORNL-3996, ORNL-4037, and ORNL-4119.
Fuel salt for the reactor is a ternary mixture consisting of about $0.3\% \times \mathrm{UF}_4$ , $65.7\% \times 7\mathrm{LiF}$ , and $34\% \mathrm{BeF}_2$ .
A salt containing $27\% \mathrm{ThF}_4$ , $71\% \mathrm{LiF}$ , and $2\% \mathrm{BeF}_2$ is proposed for the blanket.
A mixture of $48\% \mathrm{NaF}$ , $4\% \mathrm{KF}$ , and $48\% \mathrm{BF}_3$ is the favored coolant salt because of its low liquidus temperature and low cost.
Estimates of the physical properties of the salts are reported in Table 7.
A flowsheet for the 1000-Mw(e) plant is presented in Fig.
Fuel is pumped through the reactor at a rate of about 44,000 gpm, entering the core at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and leaving at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The primary fuel system has four loops, each loop having a heat exchanger and a pump of 11,000-gpm capacity.
Blanket salt circulates through each of the four loops at a rate of 2000 gpm, entering the reactor vessel at $1150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and leaving at $1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

The steam system is essentially that of the new Bull Run plant of the TVA, modified to increase the rating to 1000 Mw(e) and to preheat the working fluid to $700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ before it enters the boiler-superheaters.
Use of the supercritical steam cycle appears to ease some problems of design of steam generators for molten-salt reactors and results in a thermal efficiency of about $45\%$ .
6 shows the arrangement of equipment in the reactor and coolant cells, and a more detailed view of the reactor primary equipment is shown in Fig.
The reactor vessel is about 14 ft in diameter by about 19 ft high, is designed for $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and 150 psi and has a metal-wall thicknesses in the range of 1 to 3 in.
The reactor vessel contains a 10-ft-diam by 12-1/2-ft-high core assembly composed of 534 graphite fuel cells of a type similar to that shown in Fig.
A 1-1/2-ft-thick blanket and a 3-in.-thick graphite reflector surround the core.
The reactor vessel and all other equipment that holds salt is made of Hastelloy N, a nickel-base alloy containing about $17\%$ molybdenum, $7\%$ chromium, and $4\%$ iron.

Coolant salt flows through the shell, entering at $1125^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and leaving at $850^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Water preheated to about $700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ enters the tubes at 3800 psi and leaves as supercritical steam at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and 3600 psi.
Steam is extracted from the high-pressure turbine at about $550^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and reheated to $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and $540$ psi before use in the intermediate pressure turbine.

Since the freezing temperature of the coolant salt is about $700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , it seems desirable to preheat the working fluid to almost $700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ before it enters the boiler-superheaters or reheaters.
or an exchange process to remove Pa and $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ on a very short cycle.

During a 30-day interruption in processing of the blanket, the increase in concentration of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in the blanket salt would produce an increase of less than $20\%$ in the amount of heat generated in the blanket.
Since $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ would not be available from the blanket, the burnup in the core would have to be compensated by supplying fissile material from a reserve.
The decrease would be less than 0.02 in 30 days.
Preliminary estimates of the capital cost of a 1000-Mw(e) MSBR power station indicate a direct construction cost of about $81 million.
After applying the indirect cost factors used in the advanced converter evaluation, the estimated total plant cost is $115 million for private financing and $111 million for public financing.
A summary of plant costs is given in Table 9.

Private financing considers a capital charge rate of ${12}\%$ ’yr for depreciating capital and of ${10}\%$ ’/yr for nondeprecipitating capital; public financing considers a capital charge rate of 7%/yr for depreciating capital and 5%/yr for nondeprecipitating capital.
The reference design has four fuel circuits and four blanket circuits operating off one reactor vessel in order to produce 1000 Mw(e).
Each primary circuit of the reference design and its secondary circuits were connected to a separate reactor vessel to provide four 556-Mw(th) reactor modules.

The reference design and the modular design described above operate at the same high power density in the core and the graphite is subjected to a high dose of damaging neutrons in a few years-- $10^{23}$ neutrons/ $\mathsf{cm}^2$ (max) in four to six full-power years depending on the amount of flux flattening that can be achieved.
This dose is a factor of 4 higher than has been achieved to date in in-pile testing, and having to replace the graphite every 5 years is estimated to increase the power cost by 0:05 to 0.1 mill/kwhr.

For these reasons the first molten-salt breeder reactors are likely to be operated at lower power densities where an acceptable core life is more easily assured, so considerable attention is being given to a modular plant in which the average power density in the core is $40 \mathrm{kw} / \mathrm{liter}$ half the power density in the core of the reference design.

The reactor vessels become about 13 ft in diameter by 17 ft high; the breeding ratio remains about the same, but the yield decreases; the capital cost would be about $8\%$ higher than for the reference plant.
Some characteristics of modular plants with full and half power density in the core, with and without protactinium removal, are shown in Tables 17 and 18.
The plant factor is 0.8 as for the reference design, no credit being taken for being able to maintain a higher plant factor.
The modules can be made larger than 556 Mw(th) if desired, the capacity depending on the fraction of plant the operator is willing to have shut down for repair on short notice.
The wall separating the core and blanket would be Hastelloy N, niobium, or molybdenum, $1/8$ to $1/4$ in. thick.
The breeding performance of such a reactor is shown in Table 19.

section: PROGRAM FOR DEVELOPMENT OF MOLTEN-SALT THERMAL BREEDER POWER PLANTS
text: We believe the information in the section on fuel utilization strongly indicates the need for the U.S. to be able to build 1000-Mw(e) or larger power breeder stations of high performance by about 1980, so they could be built at a rate near 50,000 Mw(e) per year by about 1990.
It operates at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ but at 7.5 Mw(th), so the power density is low.
According to preliminary studies, the power would be 100 to 150 Mw(th).
The size would be 250 to 500 Mw(e), one module of the modular design described above.
Nuclear operation of the MSBE would begin in FY 1975.
A prototype could be in operation by 1980, and its construction would bring into being the capability for building full-scale plants.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The MSRE is a molten-salt-fueled thermal reactor that produces heat at a rate of 7.5 Mw(th) while operating at about $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: 16, and the general arrangement of the plant is shown in Fig.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The fuel for the MSRE is $65\%$ LiF-29.1% BeF2-5% ZrF4-0.9% UF4.* Except for the small amount of ZrF4 and the higher UF4 concentration, it is the fuel for the core of the reference breeder.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The flow rate is about 1250 gpm.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The MSRE normally operates at about 7.5 Mw thermal and at that power level fuel enters the reactor at $1168^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and leaves at $1210^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The base pressure in the system is 5 psig in the helium cover gas over the free surface of salt in the pump bowl.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The maximum pressure is about 55 psig at the outlet of the pump.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The heat generated in the fuel salt as it passes through the reactor vessel is transferred in the heat exchanger to a molten-salt coolant containing $66\%$ LiF and $34\%$ BeF₂.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The coolant is circulated by means of a second sump-type pump at a rate of 850 gpm through the heat exchanger, normally entering at $1015^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and leaving at $1073^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and through a radiator where the heat is dissipated to the atmosphere.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The base pressure in this system is also 5 psig in the pump tank; the maximum pressure, at the discharge of the pump, is 70 psig.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The fission product gases krypton and xenon are removed continuously from the circulating fuel salt by spraying salt at a rate of 50 gpm into the cover gas above the liquid level in the fuel pump tank.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: After a delay of about 1-1/2 hr in the piping, this gas passes through water-cooled beds of activated carbon.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The krypton and xenon are delayed until all but the $\mathbf{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ decay and then are diluted with air and discharged to the atmosphere.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: Finally, the plant is provided with a simple processing facility for treating full 75-ft³ batches of fuel salt with hydrogen fluoride and fluoro-ine gases.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The equipment approaches the size required for batchwise processing of the blanket of the 1000-Mw(e) reference reactor.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: It is about 5 ft diam by 8-1/2 ft high from the drain line at the bottom to the center of the outlet nozzle.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The wall thickness of the cylindrical section is 9/16 in.; the top and bottom heads are 1-1/8 in. thick.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The core contains approximately 600 vertical graphite bars 2 in. square x 67 in. long.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: Most of the bars have grooves 1.2 in. wide x 0.2 in. deep machined along the full length of each face.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The bars are installed with the grooves on adjacent bars aligned to form channels 1.2 in. x 0.4 in. for the salt to flow through the core.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The graphite is a new type with high strength, high density, and pore openings averaging about 0.4 microns in diameter.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The salt does not wet the graphite and cannot penetrate through the small pores unless the pressure is raised to 5 to 20 times the normal pressure in the core.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: Preliminary testing of the MSRE was begun in July, and fuel and coolant systems were heated for the first time for the prenuclear testing in the fall of 1964.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The reactor was first critical in June 1965 and reached its maximum power of about 7.5 Mw(th) in June 1966.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The accumulated operating experience through May 12, 1967, is presented in Table 20.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: Inspection of some parts of the fuel system confirmed that the corrosion was negligible during about 1890 hours of circulating salt in prenuclear and critical tests.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: Samples removed from the core showed no attack on metal or graphite during the 2760 hours of subcritical and power operation from December 1965 through July 1966.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: Some difficulty was experienced with micron-size particles of salts collecting in the tiny ports of the flow control valves, but much more difficulty was experienced after the reactor began to operate at 1 Mw with organic solids and viscous organic liquids collecting in the valves and at the entrance to the carbon beds.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: Provision is made for directing the normal seal leakage of 1 to 10 cc per day of oil to a waste tank and preventing liquid or vapor from coming in contact with the salt or cover gas in the pump tank.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The maximum power reached in the MSRE is 20 to $25\%$ below the design power.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: One day in July, 1966, when the reactor was running at full power, the power slowly decreased from $7.5\mathrm{Mw}$ to about $5.5\mathrm{Mw}$ without action on the part of the operators, and at the same time the air flow through the radiator decreased.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: Investigation soon showed that the reduction in air flow had resulted from the disintegration of the rotary element on one of the two axial blowers that operate in parallel to pump air through the radiator at a rate of about $200,000\mathrm{ft}^3/\mathrm{min}$ .

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: The blowers with new rotary elements have been operated for about 8 months with the vibrations and bearing temperatures monitored carefully.

section: Present Status of the Technology - MSRE
text: Aside from the experience with polymerization of organic materials in the off-gas system, the only unexpected behavior in the system has been that of fission products from niobium, atomic number 41, through tellurium, atomic number 52.
The maximum power density in the fuel in the power breeder is expected to be 600 to $1000\mathrm{kw / liter}$ , a factor of 20 to 35 above the maximum in the MSRE.

Results of short-term in-pile tests of fuel salt and graphite in metal capsules at 250 kw/liter and fuel salts in metal capsules at several thousand kilowatts per liter indicate that the fuel is stable and compatible with the materials at the high power density.
This graphite will be subjected to a maximum neutron dose of about $10^{23}$ nvt ( $E > 100$ kev) in five years at the high power density in the center of the core.
Whether they would survive the large radiation dose is uncertain because no graphite has yet been irradiated beyond about $3 \times 10^{22}$ nvt.

section: Criteria for the Molten-Salt Breeder Experiment
text: The average core power density in the fuel salt in the core should be at least the 470 w/liter of the MSBR reference design.

section: Criteria for the Molten-Salt Breeder Experiment
text: Results of some preliminary studies suggest that a reactor with a power level of 100 to 150 Mw(th) would satisfy these criteria.

section: Criteria for the Molten-Salt Breeder Experiment
text: Some characteristics of pilot plants of several sizes and power levels, but with an average power density of 470 w/liter in the core, are compared with those of the reference design and one module of the modular alternative in Table 21.

section: Criteria for the Molten-Salt Breeder Experiment
text: The comparison suggests that a 100- to 150-Mw(th) reactor would satisfy the criteria.
A proposed schedule for the experiment is shown in Table 22.
Conceptual design and planning would begin immediately to provide the design basis for FY-1969 authorization of Title I and part of Title 2 design for a construction project.
Authorization of construction would be requested for FY-1970.

Construction of buildings and services and procurement of major equipment would begin in FY-1971, this time being determined by the time required for parts of the final design and for essential development work.
Prenuclear testing and checkout of parts of the plant would begin in FY-1974 and the plant would reach full power in 1975.
The MSBE would be a complete power breeder plant designed to operate at 100 to 150 Mw(th) and to produce 40 to 60 Mw(e).
Preliminary estimates of the cost of the experiment and the startup are presented in Table 23.
The plant costs represent a factor of more than two escalation of costs obtained by scaling down to the experiment size the estimates for the 1000-Mw(e) MSBR and the 250-Mw(e) module.

section: Engineering Test Unit and Fuel Processing Pilot Plant
text: Serious work on the test plant is planned to begin in the middle of FY-1968 with the goal of having it in operation by the end of FY-1971.

section: Engineering Test Unit and Fuel Processing Pilot Plant
text: Operation will end in FY-1974.

Models of the pumps, the control rods, and the cover gas and xenon stripping system will be operated, solutions to other critical problems will be demonstrated, and critical parts of the heat transfer and flow tests will be completed in FY-1970.
The program is aimed at demonstrating before FY-1971 that these problems have adequate solutions.
The program is planned to provide definitive data by the end of FY-1970.

Demonstration of the continuous fluorinator and the partial decontamination of fuel salt from the MSRE in a practical vacuum still are required before FY-1971 in order to begin construction of the plant.

The program is planned to demonstrate by the end of FY-1970 the feasibility of making the essential joints in the reactor system by remote brazing or welding or by other methods proposed by the designers.
The program is planned to resolve by FY-1971 all physics questions concerning the performance of molten-salt reactors as breeders.
